question: Explain the role of maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) in training a Naive Bayes classifier for text classification.
expectedPoints: MLE is used to estimate the probability parameters from the training data.
For the class prior probability P(c), it is calculated as the proportion of documents in class c.
For the likelihood P(w|c), it is calculated as the frequency of word w in documents of class c, relative to the total word count in that class.

question: Why is add-one (Laplace) smoothing applied during the training of a Naive Bayes model? Describe a specific problem it solves.
expectedPoints: Smoothing is applied to handle words that appear in the test set but were never seen in the training set for a given class.
Without smoothing, the probability P(w|c) for an unseen word would be zero, making the entire product P(c) * ∏ P(w_i|c) zero.
Add-one smoothing adds a count of one to every word, ensuring no feature has a probability of zero and stabilizing the model.

question: What are the 'naive' assumptions made by the Naive Bayes classifier regarding the features in a text document?
expectedPoints: The 'naive' assumption is that the features (words) are conditionally independent of each other given the class label.
This means the presence or absence of a word is assumed to be unrelated to the presence or absence of any other word, given the class.
